title: Sarcocephalus sambucinus
intro: Sarcocephalus sambucinus, also known as African teaberry or Sambura, is a deciduous shrub or small tree native to tropical Africa. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antimalarial, and antimicrobial properties, which may help to alleviate fever, treat malaria, and prevent infections. Bioactive constituents of Sarcocephalus sambucinus include flavonoids, terpenoids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional medicine, the plant is often prepared as a tea, decoction, or infused in water to make a medicinal drink, and it is also used in the production of various herbal remedies, including infusions, tinctures, and capsules.
taxonomy: Sarcocephalus sambucinus is classified under the kingdom Plantae, with further classification as follows: it belongs to the family Rubiaceae, order Rubiales, and family classification is within the subfamily Cinchonoideae. This classification is based on its botanical characteristics and evolutionary relationships. The family Rubiaceae is a large family of flowering plants commonly known as the coffee or madder family, with over 600 genera. The order Rubiales is a monotypic order that includes the family Rubiaceae. Sarcocephalus sambucinus is a species within this family and order.
uses: Sarcocephalus sambucinus has been found to have various medicinal uses. The plant is traditionally used to treat fever, rheumatism, and other inflammatory conditions. Its leaves and roots are used in traditional medicine to relieve pain and reduce swelling. In some African cultures, Sarcocephalus sambucinus is used to treat respiratory infections and digestive issues. Further research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of using Sarcocephalus sambucinus for medicinal purposes.
